coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of carbon dots with bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission: Fast Gram-type identification and selective Gram-positive bacterial inactivation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jingjing
Gao, Ge
Zhang, Xiaodong
Ma, Yong-Hao
Chen, Xiaokai
Wu, Fu-Gen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of metal-free fluorescent quantum dots and can be synthesized facilely and cost-effectively. In this work, we develop a one-pot solvothermal method to prepare a novel type of quaternized CDs using two commercial reagents (glycerol and dimethyloctadecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propy]ammonium chloride, abbreviated as Si-QAC). The introduction of the long alkyl chain-containing quaternary ammonium compound Si-QAC endows the as-prepared CDs with the capability to interact with the negatively-charged bacterial cells via both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. Owning to the different surfaces of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the CDs can selectively interact with the Gram-positive bacteria. More interestingly, once the CDs are adsorbed onto the Gram-positive bacteria, their fluorescence emission is significantly enhanced. Furthermore, the CD adsorption can further cause the bacterial cell surface destruction since the CDs may change the charge balance of the bacterial surface and can insert into the bacterial surface via the long alkyl chains, which ultimately leads to the inactivation of Gram-positive bacteria. As compared with the conventional Gram-staining method, the CD-based Gram-type differentiation approach is accurate, fast, and easy-operating. Overall, the quaternized CDs represent a metal-free nanoparticle-based nanotheranostic for imaging-guided bacterial differentiation and inhibition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metal-free antibiotics
Bacterial differentiation
Bacterial imaging
Antibacterial

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of CDs on the morphology of bacteria
Distinguishment between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria
Evaluation of the interactions between CDs and bacterial cells
Evaluation of the interactions between CDs and bacterial model membranes
In vitro antibacterial activity of CDs
In vivo antibacterial activity of CDs
Selective fluorescence imaging of Gram-positive bacteria using CDs
In vitro and in vivo safety tests

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the preparation of quaternized CDs and their application for selective imaging and killing of Gram-positive bacteria. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of quaternized CDs. (A) TEM image (inset: photograph of the CD suspension) and (B) corresponding size distribution histogram. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum. (D) FTIR spectrum. (E) XPS total curve. (F) N1s, (G) C1s, and (H) Si2p high-resolution XPS curves. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs dispersed in various solvents. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A and B) Cell viability evaluation results of S. aureus and E. coli after treatment with different concentrations of CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1). (C) Colony formation results and (D) the corresponding statistical histogram of S. aureus after treatment with various CDs (5 μg/mL). (E and F) Inhibition effect of CDs(2:1) on the growth of S. aureus and E. coli as a function of incubation time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus cells without (left) or with (right) the treatment of CDs (30 μg/mL) for 2.5 h.

In vivo antibacterial performance of CDs. (A) Protocol for establishing S. aureus-infected model and quantification method of the bacterial count in the infected site. (B) Typical photographs of S. aureus-infected mice treated with PBS (control) or CD suspension. (C) Photographs and (D) statistical histogram of bacterial colonies counted from the dilutions of homogenized infected tissues. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Confocal images of three strains of Gram-positive bacteria and three strains of Gram-negative bacteria after treatment with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 1 h. (B) Flow cytometric results that quantify the fluorescence intensities of the six strains of bacteria before (control) and after treatment with CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Confocal images of the mixture of E. coli and S. aureus cells after incubation with CDs (5 μg/mL) for 1 h under 405 nm excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A and B) Fluorescence responses of CDs (5 μg/mL) after interaction with different concentrations (OD600 = 0–4) of S. aureus. (C and D) Fluorescence responses of the CDs (5 μg/mL) after interaction with different concentrations (OD600 = 0–4) of E. coli. (E and F) Fluorescence responses of the CDs (100 μg/mL) after interaction with different concentrations (0–2 mg/mL) of POPE/POPG liposomes. Shown in B, D, and F are the changes of FL peak intensities with the concentrations of bacteria or liposomes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vitro and in vivo safety evaluation results of CDs. (A) Relative viabilities of A549 and AT II cells exposed to different concentrations of CDs for 24 h. (B) Hemolysis results of different concentrations of CDs. (C) Typical H&E-stained images of major organs from PBS- (control) or CD-treated mice. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
MICs of CDs for three Gram-positive and three Gram-negative bacteria.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of carbon dots with bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission: Fast Gram-type identification and selective Gram-positive bacterial inactivation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of metal-free fluorescent quantum dots and can be synthesized facilely and cost-effectively. In this work, we develop a one-pot solvothermal method to prepare a novel type of quaternized CDs using two commercial reagents (glycerol and dimethyloctadecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propy]ammonium chloride, abbreviated as Si-QAC). The introduction of the long alkyl chain-containing quaternary ammonium compound Si-QAC endows the as-prepared CDs with the capability to interact with the negatively-charged bacterial cells via both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. Owning to the different surfaces of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the CDs can selectively interact with the Gram-positive bacteria. More interestingly, once the CDs are adsorbed onto the Gram-positive bacteria, their fluorescence emission is significantly enhanced. Furthermore, the CD adsorption can further cause the bacterial cell surface destruction since the CDs may change the charge balance of the bacterial surface and can insert into the bacterial surface via the long alkyl chains, which ultimately leads to the inactivation of Gram-positive bacteria. As compared with the conventional Gram-staining method, the CD-based Gram-type differentiation approach is accurate, fast, and easy-operating. Overall, the quaternized CDs represent a metal-free nanoparticle-based nanotheranostic for imaging-guided bacterial differentiation and inhibition.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection-related diseases afflict millions of people annually and have become an increasing threat to public health [1,2]. Since the widespread use of antibiotics in the 1940s, the lives of millions of patients have been saved. However, with the broad use and abuse of conventional antibiotics, the multidrug resistance developed by bacteria to these antibiotics has become a serious problem [1,3,4], which has accordingly prompted the requirement of developing alternative strategies to combat bacterial infections. In this context, various metal-containing nanoparticles (NPs) including silver [5,6], AuPt [7], copper [8], titanium oxide [9,10], iron oxide [11], vanadium oxide [12], molybdenum oxide [13], and zinc oxide [14], and metal-free NPs including carbon-based NPs [15–25], polymeric NPs [26–28], black phosphorus [29], and silica-based NPs [30–32], have been extensively explored for antibacterial application due to their different antibacterial mechanisms from those of classical antibiotics and their high antibacterial activities.

The high surface area to volume ratios of the antibacterial NPs enable close contact with bacterial surfaces. The antibacterial mechanisms of these NPs include damage of the respiratory chain, protein inactivation, disruption of amino acid and DNA synthesis, and destruction of cell wall/membrane. Especially, if antibacterial agents are linked to a NP, the specific nanoparticle structure allows a multivalent interaction of these antibacterial molecules with the bacterial surfaces, which is highly beneficial for enhancing the antibacterial activities of these agents [33–35]. Thus, the diverse antibacterial mechanisms of the antibacterial NPs and the potential multivalent effect endowed by the NPs ensure the successful and wide applications of these nanoagents for combating bacterial infections. However, the clinic applications of some metal-containing antibacterial NPs may be restricted by their toxic metal components and possible long-term body retention [36]. Besides, the complicated and high-cost synthesis of metal-free antibacterial NPs may also impede their clinic translation. More importantly, most of these antibacterial NPs do not have intrinsic fluorescence imaging capability. Therefore, to fabricate highly biocompatible and self-traceable NPs in facile and cost-effective ways is highly desired for promoting the clinic translation of NP-based antibacterial agents.

Carbon quantum dots (or carbon dots, CDs), which can be synthesized in a facile and cheap way, have outstanding fluorescence properties, easily tunable/modifiable surfaces, good water-dispersibility, and low/non-cytotoxicities. Up to date, the CDs have been extensively used in imaging [37–42], drug delivery [43–45], detection [46–50], light-emitting devices [51], and catalysts [52–54]. However, the antibacterial potential of CDs has only been discovered recently [18–24,55,56], with their most common antibacterial mechanism being the reactive oxygen species-induced oxidative stress, which usually involves additional devices (e.g., laser device) or agents (e.g., H2O2) [18,22,23]. We have also developed CD- and silicon NP-based quaternized NPs for bacterial inactivation and imaging [33,34]; however, these NPs are prepared in two steps and need further surface modification, which is relatively complicated and may decrease the yield and raise the cost. Therefore, to develop a CD-based nanoplatform for bacterial imaging and killing in a simpler way is becoming a focus.

On the other hand, characterizing unknown bacteria is also important for the fast diagnosis of bacterial infections. Gram staining is a common method for bacterial characterization, which can divide the bacterial species into two categories (Gram-positive and Gram-negative) [57]. However, this method includes laborious procedures and easily generates false positive results [58]. To date, several approaches such as quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction [59], standard plate count [60], and fluorescence-based methods [30,61–63] have been developed for the identification of bacterial species. However, despite the success they have achieved in many aspects, the limitations such as low reproducibility, proneness to develop unexpected drug resistance, complicated procedures, and/or long operation time significantly limit their practical applications. Therefore, facile, fast, and accurate bacterial identification methods are urgently needed.

In this research, we prepared a new kind of quaternized CDs by a one-step solvothermal treatment of two commercially available reagents—glycerol and quaternary ammonium-carrying organosilane (dimethyloctadecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propy]ammonium chloride, abbreviated as Si-QAC). The thus-obtained CDs show high antibacterial activity, excellent bacterial differentiation capability, and good water-dispersibility. A schematic illustrating the preparation process of the CDs and the applications of the CDs for selectively imaging and killing Gram-positive bacteria is shown in Fig. 1 . Glycerol, a cheap and common compound, was used as both the carbon source and reaction medium. Glycerol molecules can provide hydroxyl groups for the final CD product to ensure its good water-dispersibility. The organosilane molecule Si-QAC, containing a quaternary ammonium group and a long hydrocarbon chain, was used as the carbon source and surface passivation agent. The quaternary ammonium group and the long hydrocarbon chain of Si-QAC are crucial for endowing the final CD product with excellent antibacterial activity, since these two moieties can interact with the bacterial surface with electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. Previous reports have revealed that Si-QAC exhibits antimicrobial activity when covalently linked to a surface [64–66].

The obtained quaternized CDs exhibit concentrated local positive charges (zeta potential: ca. +33 mV), ensuring successful electrostatic interaction with negatively-charged bacterial cells. In the meantime, the high density of the bactericidal component (the long hydrocarbon chain) on the surface of the quaternized CDs enables the successful bacterial membrane insertion which significantly enhances the inhibition performance of the CDs toward the Gram-positive bacteria both in vitro and in vivo. In addition, due to the structure and composition difference of the bacterial surfaces of the Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the fluorescent quaternized CDs interact with these two types of bacteria differently, allowing selective fluorescence imaging and inactivation of Gram-positive bacteria.

Si-QAC, glycerol, acetone, dichloromethane, and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. 1-Palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine (POPE) and 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phospho-(1′-rac-glycerol) (sodium salt) (POPG) were ordered from Avanti Polar Lipids. All solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified by a Milli-Q system (Millipore).

Quaternized CDs were prepared by the solvothermal treatment of Si-QAC and glycerol. Typically, a desired volume of glycerol (5.5, 5.0, or 4.0 mL) was mixed with a desired volume of Si-QAC (0.5, 1.0, or 2.0 mL) and the obtained mixtures (∼6 mL) were degassed with nitrogen gas for 5 min to prepare quaternized CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1), respectively. After reaction at 260 °C for 4 h, the products were cooled naturally to room temperature, and the obtained CDs were then dispersed in 30 mL DI water. For each sample, the precipitate was removed by centrifugation and the supernatant was dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane with the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1 kDa for 2 days.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Malvern Nano ZS zetasizer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence spectra were collected on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL). The infrared spectra were collected with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted by a PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Ulvac-Phi). The determination of the quantum yields (QYs) of CDs was similar to a previous report [67].

Gram-negative bacteria including Proteusbacillus vulgaris (P. vulgaris), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) and Gram-positive bacteria including Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), Micrococcus luteus (M. luteus), and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were all cultured in lysogeny broth (LB) medium (10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, and 0.5 g/L NaCl). After incubation in a shaking incubator overnight, preliminary bacterial suspensions were obtained. Before treatments, the bacterial cells were brought into log phase by reinoculating the bacterial suspensions 1:50 into fresh media and cultured in the shaking incubator till the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) reached 0.5.

To study the antibacterial activity of CDs, S. aureus and E. coli cells were selected as model Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively. Diluted log phase bacterial cells (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) treated with various concentrations of CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1) were seeded in 96-well plates, respectively. After culturing in the shaking incubator at 37 °C for 2.5 h, a cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was used to determine the metabolic activities of bacterial cells. In addition, wells with LB media containing the same concentrations of CDs without bacteria were tested as control groups to eliminate the influence of CDs on the OD value. To further confirm the antibacterial activity of CDs, an adenosine triphosphate (ATP) assay was also carried out according to the previously reported method [68]. Briefly, bacterial cells were separately treated with various concentrations of CDs(2:1). After culturing for 2.5 h, an ATP detection kit (Beyotime) was used to measure the amount of ATP that correlates with the number of live cells.

S. aureus suspensions (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) were incubated with CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1) at the same final concentration of 5 μg/mL, respectively. After treatment for 2.5 h, diluted bacterial suspensions of each culture (100 μL, 1:1000 dilution) were plated onto the agar plates. The bacterial colonies formed were counted and recorded after 16 h incubation.

Bacterial suspensions (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) treated with various concentrations of CDs were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured on a shaking incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. During the culturing process, the OD600 of each well was recorded as a function of time.

The MICs of CDs were measured using the broth microdilution method. Briefly, CD suspensions at different concentrations were incubated with various types of bacteria at the final bacterial concentration of 1 × 105–1 × 106 CFU/mL over 14 h. The MIC value for the CDs toward a certain type of bacteria was obtained as the lowest CD concentration that could lead to a clear suspension (indication of no bacterial growth in the suspension).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on the morphology of bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of bacteria treated with CDs was examined using a scanning electron microscope (Ultra Plus, Zeiss, Germany). Briefly, after incubation with CDs at a final concentration of 30 μg/mL for 2.5 h, bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation and washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), followed by standard procedures for fixing and dehydrating bacterial samples, which have been described in a previous report [33].

S. aureus-infected mouse model was built to evaluate the in vivo antibacterial effect of CDs. Four-week-old female BALB/c mice (18–25 g) were purchased from Comparative Medicine Center of Yangzhou University (Jiangsu, China), and all procedures involving experimental animals were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Southeast University and were conducted in compliance with the Regulations for the Administration of Affairs Concerning Experimental Animals of China. S. aureus cells were collected by centrifugation, washed with sterile PBS, and resuspended in sterile PBS (1 × 109 CFU/mL). Then 200 μL of the above S. aureus suspension was injected subcutaneously into the mice. After in vivo infection for one day, 100 μL of CD suspension (1 mg/mL) was injected into the infected site once a day. After treatment for 2 days, all mice were sacrificed and the infected tissues were homogenized in sterile PBS (1 mL). After dilution with different factors, the tissue suspensions were plated on agar (100 μL suspension for each agar plate), and after 16 h incubation, the grown colonies were counted to analyze the number of S. aureus cells in the infected tissues of the mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinguishment between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Three strains of Gram-negative bacteria and three strains of Gram-positive bacteria were used for evaluating the bacterial imaging performance of CDs. Bacterial suspensions (about 1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) were mixed with CD-containing medium to a final CD concentration of 5 μg/mL. After incubation for 1 h, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed with PBS for three times, and then imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). The fluorescence intensities of the bacterial cells were quantified by a flow cytometer (NovoCyte 2060, ACEA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the interactions between CDs and bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the interactions between CDs and bacteria, S. aureus and E. coli cells were selected as model Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively. A series of bacterial suspensions (PBS as the solvent) were separately mixed with the CD-containing suspension (PBS as the solvent) to obtain mixtures containing different concentrations of bacterial cells (OD600 = 0–4) and a fixed CD concentration of 5 μg/mL. After incubation for 1 h, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were collected. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of mixtures containing different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, and 10 μg/mL) of CDs and a fixed concentration (OD600 = 2) of S. aureus or E. coli cells were also collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the interactions between CDs and bacterial model membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial model membranes were constructed according to the previously reported method [34,69]. Briefly, POPE and POPG were dissolved in chloroform at a molar ratio of 1:3. After drying under a nitrogen stream and vacuum, the mixture was dispersed in PBS. The bacterial liposomes were then obtained by extrusion through a polycarbonate membrane (100 nm) for 21 times. To evaluate the interaction between CDs and bacterial model membranes, a series of mixtures containing varied concentrations of liposome solutions (0–2.0 mg/mL; PBS as the solvent) and a fixed concentration of CD suspension (100 μg/mL; PBS as the solvent) were prapared. After incubation for 1 h, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were collected.

A549 (human lung cancer cells) and AT II cells (human normal lung cells) were used to test the cytotoxicity of CDs. Briefly, the cells (100 μL, 5 × 104 cells/mL) were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured overnight, and then incubated with different concentrations of CDs for 24 h. 100 μL PBS containing MTT (0.5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After incubation for 4 h, 150 μL DMSO was added to each well. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured by a microplate photometer.

A549 cells were cultured in confocal dishes at 2 × 104 cells per dish at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with CDs (10 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 2 h. Afterward, they were washed with PBS for three times to remove free CDs. Cell imaging was performed using the confocal microscope.

Fresh red blood cells (RBCs) were collected from healthy mice, washed with PBS for three times, and then diluted with PBS to prepare 4 vol% RBC stock suspension. 100 μL of the above-obtained 4 vol% RBC suspension was placed in each of the 96-well plates and mixed with 100 μL of CD suspension to reach different final CD concentrations (0, 2.5, 5, 10, or 15 μg/mL). After incubation at 37 °C for 2 h, the cell suspensions were taken out and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatants were transferred to 96-well plates and the absorbance at 540 nm was measured using a Multiskan FC microplate reader (Thermo). The RBC suspension in PBS served as the negative control, while the RBCs lysed with 1% Triton X-100 in PBS were set as the positive control. The hemolysis percentage was calculated by the following formula:
Hemolysis (%) = (mean OD540 value of treated group − mean OD540 value of negative control group)/(mean OD540 value of positive control group − mean OD540 value of negative control group) × 100%.

100 μL of CD suspension (1 mg/mL) was injected into each of the BALB/c mice once daily to test the biocompatibility of the CDs in vivo. Mice treated with PBS only were tested in parallel as a negative control. After treatment for 2 days, major organs (hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys) were separated and fixed with standard procedures. Then these tissue sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and examined using an optical microscope. All the above procedures were performed under the approval of the Animal Care Committee of Southeast University.

As shown in Fig. 1, quaternized CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method using glycerol and Si-QAC. We first characterized the CDs prepared using glycerol and Si-QAC at a volume ratio of 2:1. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2 A, the as-prepared CD suspension was highly water-dispersible. The TEM image reveals that the CDs are spherical nanoparticles with an average size of ∼3.5 nm (Fig. 2B). The average hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs measured by DLS was around 10 nm in PBS (Fig. S1). In addition, the zeta potential of the CDs was measured to be +33.0 mV in PBS, which may be attributed to the abundant quaternary ammonium groups on the surface (as will be demonstrated later), ensuring the successful electrostatic interaction between the positively-charged CDs and the negatively-charged bacterial cells.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quaternized CDs (Fig. 2C) exhibits one peak centered at around 280 nm, which may originate from the π−π* transition of CC and/or CO [70]. FTIR spectroscopy was used to identify the functional groups present on the CD surface. As shown in Fig. 2D, the broad band centered at ∼3383 cm−1 may be assigned to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. The peaks at ∼2927 (CH2 asymmetric stretching), 2850 (CH2 symmetric stretching), and 1465 cm−1 (CH2 bending) come from the long methylene chain of Si-QAC, suggesting the successful incorporation of Si-QAC molecules in the CDs. The absorption bands in the range of 1230−1000 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C−O and/or Si−O. In addition, the peaks at 1732 and 1701 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO groups, which may be formed during the dehydration and carbonization of glycerol molecules. XPS was also utilized to explore the chemical composition and functional groups of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2E, the XPS total curve indicates that the CDs contain Si, C, N, and O elements. The high-resolution curve of N1s displays two peaks at 399.6 and 402.4 eV, which can be ascribed to C–N–C and quaternary N, respectively (Fig. 2F). The C1s curve displays four peaks at 283.7, 284.5, 285.7, and 286.3 eV, which are attributed to C−Si, C−C/CC, C−N/C−O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2G). Two main peaks in the Si2p curve indicate the binding energy at 101.9 eV for Si–C bond and 102.8 eV for Si–O bond (Fig. 2H). Collectively, the FTIR and XPS results indicate that the CDs are composed of C, H, N, O, and Si elements and have various bonds/groups such as OH, CH2, C−C/CC, C−N/C−O, CO, and quaternary N. The high water-dispersibility may be due to the presence of OH and quaternary N on the surface of the CDs.

Besides, the CDs exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) from 320 to 500 nm (Fig. 3 A). The maximum PL intensity of the CDs was obtained at 473 nm under 360 nm excitation. Strong blue PL was observed from the CD suspension under a 302 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3A, inset). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the CDs was calculated to be 3.9%, using quinine sulfate (PLQY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference. The wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission property of the CDs can be attributed to the formation of surface emissive traps as well as the varying size distribution of the CDs [71–74].

In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs dispersed in several solvents (including water, ethanol, dichloromethane, and acetone) with different polarities were also collected. As shown in Fig. 3B, the CDs exhibited the weakest fluorescence emission in water and the strongest emission in ethanol. The results indicate that the fluorescence emission of the CDs was sensitive to the polarity of the solvent, and a less polar environment was highly beneficial for achieving significantly enhanced fluorescence emission of the CDs. Thus, we hypothesize that the fluorescence emission of the CDs may also increase as they interact with the bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of quaternized CDs were prepared using different volume ratios of glycerol to Si-QAC (11:1, 5:1, and 2:1), which are termed CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1), respectively. Note that unless otherwise stated, all the CDs refer to CDs(2:1) in this work. UV–vis absorption spectra of the three CDs exhibited similar absorption bands with only slight shifts in peak positions (Fig. S2), indicating the similar structures of these CDs. Besides, the color of the CD suspensions changed from yellow in CDs(11:1) to brown in CDs(2:1) (Fig. S2, inset), which may be ascribed to the increase of carbonization degree that is related to the ratio of the two reactants.

Next, S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) and E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) were chosen as model microorganisms to assess the antibacterial activities of the three CDs. As showed in Fig. 4 A, the CCK-8 assay results show that all these CDs could significantly decrease the viability of S. aureus cells in a dose-dependent manner. Among the three CDs, CDs(2:1) exhibited the strongest antibacterial activity as it could reduce the viabilities of S. aureus to 20% at a concentration as low as 2.5 μg/mL. To further confirm the antibacterial activity of CDs(2:1), we additionally used the ATP assay to measure the amount of bacterial ATP which correlates directly with the number of live bacterial cells. As shown in Fig. S3, similar results were obtained as those from the above CCK-8 assay.

The colony forming unit (CFU) counting method was also carried out to evaluate the antibacterial activities of the above three CDs. The results also reveal that CDs(2:1) have a stronger antibacterial effect compared with CDs(11:1) and CDs(5:1) (Fig. 4C and D). These data suggest that the ratio of glycerol to Si-QAC plays a significant role in the antibacterial activity of the formed CDs, and a high content of Si-QAC is important for achieving a high antibacterial activity of the resulting CDs.

For CDs(2:1), time-dependent antibacterial tests were carried out at different doses toward the Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus cells by measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600). As shown in Fig. 4E, when the concentration of CDs reached 5 μg/mL, the growth of S. aureus cells was significantly inhibited within 6 h. When the concentration of the CDs increased to 10 μg/mL, the growth of S. aureus was completely inhibited, indicating the superior antibacterial efficacy of the CDs. By contrast, for the Gram-negative bacteria E. coli cells, the CCK-8 assay results suggest that the CDs(11:1), CDs(5:1), and CDs(2:1) all exhibited weak antibacterial activities even at a high concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4B). For CDs(2:1), the time-dependent antibacterial test results also prove that the growth of E. coli cells could only be slowed down slightly at a high CD concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 4F). The above results suggest that the three quaternized CDs could selectively inhibit the growth of Gram-positive S. aureus bacteria.

To explain the selective Gram-positive bacterial inactivation property of the quaternized CDs, the cell surface composition difference of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria needs to be concerned. As for the Gram-positive bacteria, such as S. aureus cells, their surfaces are composed of a single-layered membrane with specific anionic components (lipoteichoic acids and teichoic acids) [61,75], which promote the binding of the positively-charged CDs onto the bacteria, thereby inducing an increase in membrane permeability (because of the insertion of the long alkyl chain-containing quaternary ammonium groups) and eventually leading to the cell death because of the leakage of intracellular components. However, in the case of Gram-negative bacteria, such as E. coli cells, their surfaces are composed of a double-layered membrane, and the outer one is rich in lipopolysaccharides which are cross-bridged by divalent cations [61,75], which may result in a weaker binding to quaternized CDs. In addition, the presence of small channels of porins within the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria may also block the entrance of the NPs to the bacterial cell, thus preventing the invasion of the bacteria by the CDs [76].

To generalize our conclusion and further study the different antibacterial activities of the quaternized CDs (hereafter, all the CDs refer to CDs(2:1)) toward Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the MIC values of six strains of bacteria (including three Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis and three Gram-negative bacteria E. coli, P. vulgaris, and P. aeruginosa) were measured and summarized in Table 1 . The results show that the CDs could fully inhibit the growth of all the three Gram-positive strains at very low concentrations, indicating that the Gram-positive strains are highly susceptible to the CDs. In sharp contrast, the MICs of the three Gram-negative bacteria are higher than 100 μg/mL. The results suggest the distinct interaction mechanisms of the CDs and the two types of bacteria and confirm the superb antibacterial activity of the CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria. In addition, we compared the MIC values of CDs with those of several other antibacterial agents (including common antibiotics), and the corresponding data listed in Table S1 further highlight the excellent antibacterial activity of the CDs toward the Gram-positive S. aureus cells.

To gain insight into the antibacterial mechanism of the CDs, the morphological changes of bacteria before or after treatment with the CDs were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 5 , both S. aureus and E. coli bacteria showed clear edges and intact cell walls before CD treatment; whereas, after CD treatment, cellular deformation and membrane collapse could be clearly seen in S. aureus cells. Meanwhile, the leakage of intracellular contents could be observed for many of the S. aureus cells, suggesting that the antibacterial mechanism of the CDs should involve the bacterial cell wall and membrane disruption, similar to a previous report [77]. By contrast, for E. coli, most of the cells remained intact in morphology after CD treatment, indicating the negligible bacterial disruption capability of the CDs.

The above SEM results may be explained by the different interaction modes between the CDs and the two types of bacterial cells. We believe that the CDs can effectively disrupt the cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria through the synergistic action of both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions as follows: First, the positively-charged CDs are attracted to come close with and bind to the negatively-charged bacteria through the strong electrostatic interaction. After bacterial binding, the CDs can further interact with the cytoplasmic membrane through membrane insertion using their long hydrocarbon chains, which may lead to the membrane damage and increase in membrane permeability. Finally, the accumulated membrane damage induces leakage of intracellular contents followed by bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the in vivo antibacterial activity of CDs, S. aureus-infected mice were utilized as a model to assess the in vivo therapeutic efficacy of the CDs toward bacterial infection (Fig. 6 A). The mice were divided into two groups: PBS- and CD-treated groups, respectively (Fig. 6B). In this study, therapeutic efficacy was assessed by quantifying and comparing the bacterial counts in the infected site (Fig. 6C). As shown in Fig. 6D, after the treatment, quantification of bacterial colonies in the infected tissue showed 4.24 × 108 CFU/g for the PBS-treated group. By contrast, the CD-treated group showed a remarkable reduction in the bacterial load, with only 1.9% surviving fraction compared with that of the PBS-treated group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescence imaging of Gram-positive bacteria using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: With their multicolor fluorescence emission and especially the polarity-sensitive fluorescence emission properties, the CDs may realize bacterial imaging after bacterial adsorption. To confirm this, confocal microscopy was utilized to evaluate the imaging performance of the CDs on the six strains of bacteria (three Gram-positive bacteria and three Gram-negative bacteria). Typically, the structural difference of bacteria led to the different fluorescence responses, as shown in Fig. 7 A. Besides, after culturing with CDs for 1 h, multi-color fluorescence signals from CDs were observed in all the three Gram-positive strains under different excitation wavelengths; whereas no noticeable fluorescence signals were observed in the three Gram-negative strains under the same conditions. The above results indicate that the CDs could light up Gram-positive bacteria selectively.

Flow cytometric experiments were also carried out to quantitatively analyze the fluorescence intensities of the CD-treated bacteria. According to the flow cytometric results presented in Fig. 7B, after treatment with CDs for 1 h, the fluorescence intensities of the three Gram-positive strains were significantly enhanced, which were consistent with the confocal imaging results. For Gram-negative strains, the fluorescence intensities of the CD-treated groups exhibited negligible variations compared with the untreated groups. The confocal imaging results as well as the flow cytometric results both confirm the selective imaging ability of the CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria, indicating that the fluorescent CDs may serve as a nanoplatform for fluorescence imaging-based differentiation of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

To further investigate the selective recognition ability of the CDs more intuitively, a mixture of S. aureus and E. coli cells incubated with the CDs was visualized by confocal imaging. As shown in Fig. 8 , the spherical S. aureus cells showed a much higher fluorescence intensity than the rod-shaped E. coli cells, suggesting that the CD staining method is a rapid and simple alternative to the traditional Gram staining method for differentiating Gram-positive bacteria from Gram-negative ones.

As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence emission of the CDs was sensitive to the polarity of the solvent, and a less polar environment was highly beneficial for achieving the significantly enhanced fluorescence emission of the CDs. Thus, we hypothesize that the selective imaging capability of the CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria may be attributed to the different interaction modes between CDs and the Gram-positive/Gram-negative bacteria. We speculate that the fluorescence emission of the CDs increases when they bind to the Gram-positive bacterial cells. To confirm our hypothesis, the Gram-positive S. aureus and the Gram-negative E. coli cells were selected as model bacteria to see if they can lead to the fluorescence intensity changes of the CDs. Fig. 9 A and C show the emission spectra of the CD suspensions at a fixed concentration of 5 μg/mL after mixing with different concentrations of S. aureus and E. coli cells, respectively, and the corresponding bacterial concentration-dependent fluorescence intensities at 500 nm are shown in Fig. 9B and D, respectively. The results confirm that only the Gram-positive S. aureus cells led to the fluorescence intensity increase of the CDs, while the Gram-negative E. coli cells did not change the fluorescence emission of the CDs, revealing the contact-enhanced fluorescence emission property of CDs toward Gram-positive bacteria. To further testify the above conclusion, we have also tested the fluorescence intensities at 500 nm of CDs at different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, and 10 μg/mL) after mixing with S. aureus and E. coli cells at a fixed concentration (OD600 = 2), respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensities of the CD–S. aureus mixtures were higher than those of the CD–E. coli mixtures at the same CD concentrations. The above results confirm the Gram-positive bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission property of CDs, which may be attributed to the different cell surface structures and compositions of the two types of bacteria. It has been reported that Gram-positive bacteria are more sensitive to lipophilic molecules than Gram-negative ones [78]. Similarly, in our case, the positively-charged CDs are prone to bind to the Gram-positive bacterial surfaces, which enables the penetration of CDs into the less polar cytoplasmic membranes and finally improves the fluorescence imaging performance of CDs.

To further confirm our hypothesis, we prepared the model membranes of Gram-positive bacteria—liposomes made of POPE and POPG at a molar ratio of 1:3 [34]. The average hydrodynamic diameter of the obtained liposomes was 124.5 ± 20.1 nm with a polydispersity index of 0.133 as measured by DLS. As shown in Fig. 9E and F, we found that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (at a fixed concentration of 100 μg/mL) significantly increased after incubation with increasing liposomal concentration (from 0 to 2 mg/mL) for 1 h, suggesting that the CDs could effectively interact with the Gram-positive bacterial model membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo safety tests
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro and in vivo safety of the CDs was also evaluated since it is important for the biomedical applications of the material. First, cytotoxicity and hemolysis experiments were carried out to evaluate the in vitro biocompatibility of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 10 A, after treatment with 5 μg/mL CDs for 24 h, more than 80% of both A549 and AT II cells remained alive. However, as shown in Fig. 4A, over 98% of the S. aureus cells were killed within 2.5 h at the same CD dose. The results suggest that the CDs are a promising antibacterial agent with low toxicity to mammalian cells. Such a selective cell killing property of the cationic CDs is mainly because that the mammalian cells are less negatively-charged than the bacterial cells [79]. Moreover, considering the excellent compatibility of the CDs toward the mammalian cells, we collected the confocal fluorescence images of the CD-treated A549 cells to test if the CDs can be used for cell imaging. The intense fluorescence signals emitted from the treated cells under different laser excitations (Fig. S5) prove the above deduction.

Next, the hemolytic activity of the CDs was also tested. Red blood cells (RBCs) in PBS (pH = 7.4) and in 1% Triton X-100 solution were used as negative control and positive control, respectively. Similar to the MTT results, negligible hemolytic activity was observed for quaternized CDs at concentrations up to 15 μg/mL (Fig. 10B).

Last, we evaluated the in vivo toxicity of the CDs. The major organs including hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys were collected from mice after PBS (control) or CD treatment and stained with H&E. The tissues from mice treated with CDs remained undisturbed compared with those from mice in the control group (Fig. 10C), demonstrating the good in vivo biosafety of the CDs.

In summary, we have prepared a new type of quaternized CDs using one-step solvothermal treatment of two commercial reagents, glycerol and Si-QAC. The CDs with good aqueous dispersity and strong fluorescence emission could selectively interact with the Gram-positive bacteria, lighting up the bacteria with their multicolor fluorescence emission and inactivating the bacteria both in vitro and in vivo via disrupting the bacterial walls/membranes. Compared with other metal-based antibiotic nanoparticles, the metal-free CDs have the advantages of low-cost and facile synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, and self-traceable capability. We believe that the intrinsic fluorescence and the bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission property of the CDs may enable the rapid and accurate imaging-guided identification and counting of Gram-positive bacteria, which may have great potential for bacterial infection-oriented theranostics.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21673037).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
supplementary material-proof supplementary material-proof

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.02.040.
